title: How to Use Scrying for Psychic Development
meta_description: Struggling with scrying isn't a failure. It's a skill you build. This guide strips away the mystique and shows you how to use scrying for real psychic development. No velvet tents required.
og_title: How to Use Scrying for Psychic Development
og_description: Struggling with scrying isn't a failure. It's a skill you build. This guide strips away the mystique and shows you how to use scrying for real psychic development. No velvet tents required.
body:
Wait, Scrying Isn't Just Crystal Balls and Fortune Telling?

Let's get one thing straight right now. When I say "scrying," you probably picture a woman in a velvet tent with a crystal ball, telling people they're going to meet a tall, dark stranger. And sure, that's a version of it. But that's like saying cooking is just boiling water. There's so much more going on under the surface.

Scrying is one of the oldest psychic tools we have. It's been used for thousands of years, in every culture, by people who needed to see what wasn't obvious. And here's the thing - you've probably already done it without knowing. You've stared into a campfire and gotten lost in the flames. You've looked at clouds and seen faces. You've watched water ripple in a glass and felt something shift in your chest. That's scrying. You're already wired for it.

So why do so many people struggle with it? Because they think they have to "see" something, like a movie playing in their head. They think they need special gifts or years of training. They think if they're not getting clear visions, they're failing. None of that is true. Right?!

Scrying works because your brain is designed to find patterns in chaos. It's called pareidolia - you know, when you see a face in a piece of toast. Scrying just points that natural ability in a specific direction and asks your intuition to fill in the gaps. You're not making things up. You're seeing what's already there, just below the surface.

What Actually Happens When You Scry

Here's the honest truth: scrying is a trick. Not a dishonest trick - a trick on your own brain. You look at something with a lot of visual noise - water, smoke, crystal, a dark mirror - and your conscious mind gets bored. It can't make sense of the chaos. So it hands the controls over to your deeper mind. That's where the psychic stuff lives.

When you're staring at a black mirror or a bowl of water, your logical brain says, "I don't know what I'm looking at." And then it gives up. It stops trying to label everything. And in that space, your intuition can speak. You might get an image, a feeling, a word, a memory that seems unrelated. You might feel a temperature change or hear a sound that isn't there. That's the information coming through.

Most people quit because they expect too much too fast. They want a full-color vision with surround sound. But psychic information is more like a whisper in a crowded room. You have to learn to listen for it. And scrying is the practice that teaches you how to do that.

"You are not making up what you see in the water. You are finally allowing yourself to see what you've always known."

What You Need to Get Started (Spoiler: Almost Nothing)

You don't need expensive tools. You don't need a special room or candles or incense. You need a surface to look at, some quiet time, and a willingness to be wrong. That last part is the hardest. Most of us are terrified of being wrong. But in scrying, being "wrong" is just part of the process. You're learning a new language. You're going to mispronounce things.

Here are the most common scrying mediums, starting with the easiest:

  • Water. A simple bowl of water. Darken the room, put a light behind the bowl so the surface catches it. That's it. You can add a drop of ink or food coloring if you want, but clear water works fine.
  • A dark mirror. You can buy one or make one by painting the back of a glass picture frame with black paint. What you're looking for is the reflection of the dark space, not your own face. This is powerful because it has no visual texture - your brain has nothing to latch onto.
  • Crystal ball. Yes, the classic. But don't buy a cheap one. You want clear quartz or beryl, not glass. The internal fractures and bubbles in a real crystal give you something to watch. Glass is too perfect - it doesn't create enough visual noise.
  • Smoke. From incense, a candle, or a fire. Watch the movement, the shapes, the way it curls. This is more dynamic and can be harder to hold steady, but some people connect with it immediately.
  • Fire. Staring into flames is ancient. The movement, the color shifts, the way the wood breaks down - it's hypnotic. Your brain drops into a different state almost instantly.

If you want a solid starting point, I recommend a simple dark mirror. It's portable, it doesn't spill, and it gives you a lot of room to work. You can find a good one See on Amazon (paid link) if you don't want to make your own. The key is having a deep black surface that reflects nothing but darkness.

How to Actually Do It (Step by Step, No Fluff)

Let's walk through a session. Put your phone away. Turn off notifications. This is not a multitasking activity. You can't scry while half-watching TV. Your brain needs to settle.

Step 1: Set up your space. You want low light. Not complete darkness - you need to see your surface. A single candle off to the side is perfect. Place your mirror or bowl so you're comfortable. You might sit for 10-20 minutes, so don't strain your neck.

Step 2: Ground yourself. Take three deep breaths. Feel your feet on the floor. Feel the weight of your body in the chair. You don't need a ritual. Just be present. If your mind is running through your to-do list, you're not ready. Wait until you feel a little more settled.

Step 3: Soften your gaze. Don't stare hard. Don't try to focus. Let your eyes go slightly out of focus. You're not looking at the surface - you're looking into it. This is the hardest part for beginners. You want to see the whole field at once, not zoom in on one spot.

Step 4: Watch and wait. At first, you'll see nothing. Maybe some light reflections, maybe your own breath fogging the mirror. Your mind will say, "This is stupid. Nothing is happening." That's normal. Stay with it. After a few minutes, you might notice the surface seems to move. The darkness might look like it's swirling. The water might look like it's breathing. That's the altered state beginning.

Step 5: Let images come. You might see shapes forming - faces, landscapes, symbols, letters. Don't grab at them. Don't try to analyze. Just notice. "I see something that looks like a tree." That's it. Don't ask what it means yet. Just catalog what you're seeing. If nothing comes, that's fine. Sometimes you get feelings instead of images. You might feel cold, or sad, or excited. That's also information.

Step 6: Close the session. When you feel done, look away. Blink a few times. Move your body. Write down everything you saw, felt, or thought - even the things that seemed stupid. Especially the things that seemed stupid. Those are often the most important.

That's it. That's the whole practice. You do this regularly, and you get better. Not because you open up some secret power, but because you train your brain to trust what it's receiving.

What the Heck Do the Images Mean?

This is where most people get stuck. They see a snake in the water and immediately think, "Oh no, betrayal! Danger!" But symbols don't work like that. A snake might mean transformation to you. It might mean healing (the medical symbol). It might mean you watched a nature documentary last night and your brain is just recycling images. Context matters.

Here's the rule: the first interpretation that comes to mind is usually the right one for you. Your intuition already knows what the symbol means. Your logical brain wants to look it up in a book or Google it. Don't. Trust your gut. If you see a key and you immediately think "opportunity," that's your answer. If you see a key and you feel sad, maybe it's about something you've lost. The symbol is just a carrier for the feeling.

Keep a journal. Write down what you saw, what you felt, and what you thought it meant. Then check back later. You'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that certain symbols show up before specific events. Your personal symbol dictionary builds itself over time. No one else can tell you what your snake means.

If you want a structured way to track your scrying sessions, See on Amazon (paid link) has a great journal format that a lot of my students use. It helps you stay consistent without overthinking it.

Why Scrying Works for People Who "Can't Visualize"

I hear this all the time: "I can't see anything in my mind's eye. I'm not a visual person." And honestly, that might make you better at scrying than someone who is. Here's why.

People who visualize easily often get caught in their own heads. They start generating images internally and think they're seeing something in the mirror. But they're just daydreaming. If you're not a natural visualizer, you're less likely to confuse imagination with perception. You're more likely to see what's actually in front of you.

Scrying is an external practice. You're looking at something outside yourself. The images appear on the surface, not in your head. Yes, your brain is projecting them, but the process is different. You're not trying to conjure something. You're receiving something. That distinction matters.

If you're not visual, you might get information in other ways. You might feel a temperature shift when you look at a certain part of the mirror. You might hear a word or a sound. You might get a sudden urge to move your body in a certain way. All of that is valid. Don't force yourself to see pictures if that's not how you work.

Common Mistakes That Make People Give Up

Let me save you some frustration. Here are the biggest traps beginners fall into:

  • Trying too hard. Scrying is a passive practice. You can't force it. If you're straining, you're doing it wrong. Relax your eyes, relax your jaw, relax your shoulders. The information comes when you're soft, not when you're rigid.
  • Expecting Hollywood visions. You're not going to see a full scene with dialogue and a soundtrack. That's a movie. Real scrying is subtle. It's a flash of a face. A shape that looks like a letter. A feeling of dread or peace. The big visions come with practice, but even then, they're not the norm.
  • Analyzing while you're working. Save the interpretation for after. While you're scrying, just receive. Let the images flow. If you stop to think, "What does that mean?" you break the state. You have to be a receiver first and a detective second.
  • Not writing it down. I cannot stress this enough. Your memory is garbage. You'll think you remembered everything, and an hour later it's gone. Write it down immediately. Even if it's just a few words. Future you will thank you.
  • Giving up too soon. The first session is usually the hardest. Your brain doesn't know what to do. Give it at least five sessions before you decide if scrying is for you. That's a week of practice. You can do that.

How to Know If You're Making It Up

This is the question everyone wants to ask but feels embarrassed about. "How do I know I'm not just imagining things?"

Here's the honest answer: sometimes you are. And that's okay. The line between imagination and intuition is blurry, especially at first. But there are ways to tell the difference.

Imagination feels like you're creating. It feels active. You're deciding what to see. Intuition feels like you're receiving. It feels passive. You're noticing what's already there. Imagination is usually logical - it makes sense. Intuition is often weird - it doesn't fit neatly into your worldview.

Also, imagination tends to be boring. It gives you what you expect. Intuition surprises you. If you're seeing things that make you go, "Huh, that's weird," that's probably real. If you're seeing things that make you go, "Yeah, that makes sense," you're probably making it up.

Does that land? Good. Because here's the secret: even experienced psychics make things up sometimes. The goal isn't to be perfect. The goal is to get better at telling the difference. And the only way to do that is to practice, make mistakes, and learn from them.

Taking It Deeper: Scrying for Specific Questions

Once you're comfortable with general scrying, you can start asking questions. This is where it gets interesting. You can scry for guidance on relationships, career, health, anything. But you have to ask the right way.

Bad question: "Will I get the job?" That's a yes/no question, and scrying doesn't do yes/no well. It's not a Magic 8-Ball.

Good question: "Show me what I need to know about this job opportunity." That's open-ended. That gives your intuition room to show you something useful. Maybe you see a closed door, which suggests it's not the right path. Maybe you see a light, which suggests potential. Maybe you see nothing, which might mean you're not ready to know yet.

Ask your question out loud or in your head before you start. Then scry normally. Don't try to force an answer. Just watch what comes. The answer might not be literal. You might see a symbol that relates to something completely different. Trust the process.

If you want to work with a more structured approach to scrying and psychic development, See on Amazon (paid link) is a book I recommend to my students who want to go deeper. It gives you a approach without making it feel like homework.

A Final Word on Fear

Some people are scared of scrying. They think they'll see something dark or open a door they can't close. Let me put your mind at ease. You are not going to summon anything. You are not going to see something that damages you. The images you see come from your own subconscious. They're already inside you. Scrying just lets you look at them in a new way.

If something scary comes up, it's not a demon. It's a part of you that needs attention. Maybe it's fear, maybe it's a memory, maybe it's a warning. Treat it with respect, but don't worship it. You're the one in control. You can look away anytime. You can close the session anytime. You are safe.

Scrying is a tool for clarity, not for fear. It's a way to see what's already there. And what's there is usually not as scary as your imagination makes it. The things we fear in the dark are almost always just shadows of our own making.

So get a bowl of water. Get a dark mirror. Get a candle. Sit down. Soften your eyes. Watch. And see what happens. You might be surprised at what you find.

And if you want more guidance on where to start with your psychic development, take this quick assessment to find out what practice fits your natural style: Take the Psychic Style Quiz or check out our free starter assessment.
